root: ^The night passes slowly. Adam and Eve sit together — truly together again, for the first time since the fruit — in the bower that has been their home since the beginning of the world. They do not sleep. Sleep, that gentle gift that once came as easily as breathing, now evades them. Their minds are too full. Their hearts are too heavy.

^Adam stares at the stars through the canopy of leaves. The same stars. The same sky. But he sees them differently now — as vast, cold, impossibly distant. He used to feel the stars were his neighbours. Now they are strangers.
^In the darkest hour before dawn, the angel Michael descends.

^He comes in full glory — not the gentle presence of the evening patrols, but the archangel of war, Heaven's general, blazing with a light so fierce that the garden's night-blooming flowers close against it. In his hand he carries a sword that burns.
^They rise. They know why he has come. The sentence has been passed. The garden is no longer theirs.
^But Michael does not lead them immediately to the gates. Instead, he turns to Adam with something that might, in an angel's face, be called compassion.

^Before you leave this place, God grants you a gift. I will show you what is to come — the future of your children, and their children, and all the generations that will spring from this moment. Look, and understand what your choice has wrought.
^(Adam accepts. He owes it to the future to witness what he has caused.)
^(Adam recoils. He does not want to see the suffering he has unleashed.)

c-0: ^Show me. I chose this — I will not turn away from what follows.
^Michael nods. He touches Adam's eyes, and the veil of time parts like a curtain.
c-1: ^Adam steps back. His hands rise, as if to shield his eyes from something already visible.
^I am afraid. What if I cannot bear it?
^Then you will learn to bear it. As your children will.
^Adam closes his eyes. Opens them. Nods, once.

the_visions: ^Adam sees Cain and Abel — his own sons, not yet born, not yet named. He sees the altar, the offerings, the jealousy that curdles in Cain's heart like milk left in the sun. He sees the rock. He sees the blood. He sees the first murder — brother killing brother in a field of golden wheat.

^The vision shifts. Adam sees cities rising from the earth — Enoch, Babel, the great works of human ambition. He sees invention — tools, writing, music, the first songs sung by human voices. He sees beauty that takes his breath away.

^But he also sees war. Famine. Plague. He sees the strong crushing the weak. He sees children crying in the ruins of cities. He sees the earth itself growing corrupted, heavy with violence and greed, until God's patience reaches its limit.
^A wall of water rising above the mountains. A world drowned. Every living thing — the animals he named, the gardens he tended, the children of his children — swept away. All of it. Gone.
^(He weeps. The weight of it is unbearable.)
^(He endures. He forces himself to watch every moment.)

c-0: ^The tears come — not the quiet tears of Eden, where sadness was a passing cloud, but great, wracking sobs that shake his entire body. He weeps for Cain and Abel. He weeps for the cities that will rise and fall. He weeps for the drowned world and the animals that trusted him and the children he will never meet.
^Is there no hope? Is this all there is — suffering upon suffering, death upon death, forever?
^Michael waits. Then, gently, he shows the rest.
c-1: ^Adam's jaw tightens. His eyes stay open. He watches the Flood swallow the world, and he does not look away. He owes the dead that much — to witness what his choice set in motion, to carry the weight of it without flinching.
^There is more. There is always more.

the_promise: ^Michael shows Adam the rainbow after the Flood. The covenant — God's promise never to destroy the world by water again. He shows Noah's family stepping onto dry land, beginning again, carrying the seed of humanity forward into an empty world.
^He shows Abraham on the mountain, the knife raised, the ram caught in the thicket. Faith tested to its breaking point and found sufficient.
^He shows Moses before the burning bush. The plagues of Egypt. The parting of the sea. The law given on Sinai — God reaching down to humanity again, not in punishment but in guidance.
^And then Michael shows Adam something that makes the archangel's voice falter — the only time in the vision that Michael himself seems moved.
^A child. Born in poverty, in a stable, under a star. A man who walks among the sick and the broken. A cross on a hill. A tomb, empty.
^What is this? Who is this man?
^He is the seed of the woman. The one promised in the garden — the one who will bruise the serpent's head. He is...
^Michael pauses. Even angels struggle with this.
^He is God's answer to what happened here today. He will take your sin upon Himself. He will die the death that was pronounced on you. And through His death, death itself will die.
^(Adam understands — this is grace. Unearned, undeserved, freely given.)
^(Adam does not fully understand — but he trusts. That is enough.)

c-0: ^The vision fades. Adam stands in the garden again — the real garden, the present garden, with its dew and its birdsong and the first grey light of dawn creeping over the eastern wall.
^He turns to Michael. His face is wet with tears, but his eyes are clear.
^O goodness infinite, goodness immense! That all this good of evil shall produce, and evil turn to good — more wonderful than that which by creation first brought forth light out of darkness!

^It is the first theology. The first attempt by a human mind to make sense of the impossible — that God can bring good from evil, that suffering can be redeemed, that the Fall is not the end of the story.
c-1: ^The vision fades. Adam stands blinking in the garden, the future still echoing behind his eyes. He does not understand all of it — how could he? He has seen thousands of years compressed into moments, civilisations rising and falling like breaths.

^But he understands enough. He understands that the story does not end here. That something is coming — someone — who will make right what he and Eve have made wrong. He does not know how. He does not know when. But he trusts.
^I do not understand everything you have shown me. But I have seen enough to know that God has not abandoned us. And that is enough.

the_departure: ^Michael leads Adam and Eve to the eastern gate of Eden. The gate they entered through — was it only days ago? Weeks? Time has become strange; they have not yet learned to measure it by sorrow.

^Eve walks beside Adam. She has been quiet through the visions — Michael showed them only to Adam — but she has watched her husband's face, and she has read the future there as clearly as if she had seen it herself.
^Adam. Whatever comes — we face it together. As we always have.
^Adam takes her hand. He squeezes it once, hard.
^They reach the gate. The eastern wall of Eden rises before them — that boundary between Paradise and the world outside, which they have never crossed, never needed to cross, never imagined crossing.
^Michael raises his burning sword. The gate opens.

^Beyond it lies the world. The real world — not the garden, not Paradise, not the curated beauty of God's enclosed experiment in innocence. The real world, with its thorns and its winters and its vast, indifferent distances.
^It is terrifying. It is beautiful. It is theirs.
^(They step through together, hand in hand. They do not look back.)
^(They step through. Eve looks back once. Adam pulls her gently forward.)
^(They pause on the threshold. One last breath of Eden's air. Then they go.)

c-0: ^They walk through the gate and they do not look back. Not because it is easy — it is the hardest thing either of them has ever done — but because looking back would make the leaving impossible, and the leaving must be done.

^Behind them, Michael stations the Cherubim — blazing angels with swords of fire that turn every way, guarding the entrance forever. The Tree of Life stands just beyond the gate, its fruit glowing with eternal life that is no longer theirs to claim.
^The gate closes. Eden vanishes behind walls of light and fire and angelic steel.
c-1: ^They cross the threshold. Eve stops. She turns — just for a moment — and looks back at Eden.

^She sees the bower where they slept. The roses she tended. The river where she first saw her own reflection and reached for it, thinking it was another creature, before God's voice called her away to something better.
^Her eyes fill with tears. Then Adam's hand tightens around hers, and she turns away from Paradise for the last time.
^Behind them, the Cherubim descend — vast, terrible, beautiful, their flaming swords carving arcs of fire across the entrance. The way back is sealed.
c-2: ^They stop at the threshold. The world outside is grey in the pre-dawn light — hills and rocks and scrubby vegetation, so different from Eden's cultivated perfection that it looks like another planet.

^Adam breathes in. The air of Eden — that sweet, perfect, flower-scented air — fills his lungs one last time. Eve closes her eyes and listens to the garden's sounds: the birdsong, the water, the whisper of wind through leaves that will never know autumn.
^Then they step through. And the gate closes behind them with a sound like the last page of a book turning.
^The Cherubim take their positions. The flaming swords ignite. Eden is sealed — forever and for all time, until the world is made new.

the_end: ^The world before them is vast and unknown. The sun is rising — the same sun that rose over Eden, but here it feels different. Harder. More distant. More honest.

^Adam and Eve stand on a hillside overlooking a plain that stretches to the horizon. There are no paths. There are no gardens. There is only the raw, unformed world, waiting for human hands to shape it.

^Eve shivers. The air outside Eden is cooler than anything she has ever felt. Adam puts his arm around her — an old gesture, but it means something new now. Not just affection but protection. Not just love but solidarity against a world that will not be kind.

^They begin to walk. Their steps leave the first human footprints in the soil outside Paradise — small marks in the dust that the wind will erase by nightfall. But more will follow. And more. Until the whole earth is covered with the tracks of Adam's children, walking the world their parents were given and lost and must now, step by painful step, reclaim.
^Some natural tears they dropped, but wiped them soon. The world was all before them, where to choose their place of rest, and Providence their guide.
^They, hand in hand, with wandering steps and slow, through Eden took their solitary way.
